text:
Possibly the strangest event atParis2024 –which is saying something– surfing is anOlympicsport unlike any other.

After many years of campaigning, surfing was introduced to the Games at Tokyo 2020, with competitors performing manoeuvers and tricks on huge waves which are scored by judges.

The scores are based on the variety, type and difficulty of the tricks as well as the surfers’ speed, power and how seamlessly they connect their moves from one to the next.

Its inclusion in Paris has raised eyebrows however, so here’s everything you need to know about Surfing at the Olympics:

Firstly – as you might have guessed – the surfing will not be taking place in Paris. Or France for that matter.

Instead, it will be held at inTeahupo’o, Tahiti, the largest island in the French overseas territory of Polynesia in the south Pacific Ocean. Rather ominously, Teahupo’o translates to ‘wall of skulls’.

Chosen for it’s massive 10ft high waves, Teahupo’o is around 15,000km (9,300 miles) from Paris – the greatest distance an Olympic event has been held from its host city.

Team GBSurfing’s performance coach Luke Dillon has highlighted the dangers of surfing at this location, telling theGuardian: ‘It’s one of the scariest waves in the world and it’s in the Pacific so it’s very powerful.

‘It’s an insane feeling to surf premium-sized waves like that. First you need skill and bravery to drop in, then instinct kicks in. It’s like dropping into a vert-ramp.’

The surfing is slated to take place between27-31 July, with the medal matches being held on the last day. However, this is all weather permitting, with the following five days being allocated as reserve days if need be.

Given it’s location, the event won’t get underway until 6pm UK time each evening, with the gold medal matches not scheduled to get underway until 2:34am on the 31 July.

Full details of the TV schedule are to be revealed, butBBCwill have coverage of all 32 Olympic events on BBC One, BBC Two and BBC iPlayer. Additionally,Eurosportare showing some sports live.

Sadly, there areno members of Team GBamong the 48 men and women competing in surfing, though Sky Brown, who won bronze in skateboarding in Tokyo, could get a wildcard spot if there is a late dropout.

In the men’s event, keep an eye on three-time world champion Gabriel Medina of Brazil and two-time world champion John John Florence of the USA, as well as Australia’s Jack Robinson.

In the women’s event, reigning gold medallist and five-time world champion Marissa Moore of the USA is the one to beat, while France’s Vahiné Fierro, who was born in French Polynesia, can expect plenty of home support.

Obviously, the surfers and officials aren’t staying in the Olympic Village in Paris. Instead organisers have hired the 126m-longAranui 5 cruise shipto be docked off the island, making it the first ever floating Olympic village.

Typically, the Aranui 5 takes passengers on a 12-day cruise of the various French Polynesia at a cost upwards of £4,074 per person.

title: Olympics 2024 surfing: From beastly waves to a floating athletes village | Metro News
description: Everything you need to know about surfing at the Paris 2024 Olympic Games; where is it taking place, how to watch and who are the key names?
